metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config-key location conflict: pick one canonical home (LLMConfig/[llm]/REBAR_LLM_* OR VerifyConfig/[verify]/REBAR_VERIFY_*), update the 'Config keys' section, AC4, AC6, and the Files list to agree on the same class, table, env-var prefix, and source file.
findings.finding: The config key location is contradicted between the body and the ACs/Files list. The 'Config keys' section says keys live on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, read from the `[llm]` table via `_llm_int(...)` with `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars. AC6 explicitly says the keys exist on `VerifyConfig`, read from `[verify]`. AC4 references `verify.llm_retry_max_wait_s`. The Files list names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (the VerifyConfig home) as the target file, not `src/rebar/llm/config.py`. An executor cannot proceed without stopping to ask which is correct: implementing on LLMConfig breaks AC6, implementing on VerifyConfig contradicts the body's rationale.
findings.location: Section 'Config keys (added to LLMConfig)' vs AC6 vs Files list
findings.suggested_fix: The body's rationale ('every LLM operational knob lives on LLMConfig') is stronger and more consistent with the existing codebase pattern. Accept that decision, then update AC6 to read 'llm_retry_max_attempts / llm_retry_max_wait_s exist on LLMConfig, are read from [llm] with REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS / REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S env fallbacks', change AC4's reference from `verify.llm_retry_max_wait_s` to `cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s`, and update the Files list to name `src/rebar/llm/config.py` instead of `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`.  If VerifyConfig is genuinely intended, do the reverse: update the body text to drop 'NOT VerifyConfig' and align the env-var prefix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the direct contradiction between the body (LLMConfig / src/rebar/llm/config.py / REBAR_LLM_* / [llm] table) and AC item 6 + Files (VerifyConfig / src/rebar/_config_schema.py / [verify] table) — pick one authoritative location and update all three sites (body config-key section, AC 6, and the Files list) to agree.
findings.finding: The plan body explicitly places `llm_retry_max_attempts` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s` on `LLMConfig` (in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`), read from the `[llm]` TOML table via `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars. But AC item 6 says these keys exist on `VerifyConfig`, are read from `[verify]`, and the Files list names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`. An implementer following the body and an implementer following the ACs would produce different code in different files with different env var names.
findings.location: Plan body § 'Config keys' vs AC item 6 vs Files list
findings.suggested_fix: If the rationale in the body is correct (LLM operational knobs belong on LLMConfig), update AC 6 to say '`llm_retry_max_attempts` / `llm_retry_max_wait_s` exist on `LLMConfig`, read from `[llm]` via `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` / `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`' and remove `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` from the Files list (or note it is untouched). Conversely, if VerifyConfig is correct, update the body to drop the 'NOT VerifyConfig' statement and the `_llm_int` / `REBAR_LLM_*` references.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the LLMConfig-vs-VerifyConfig contradiction: pick ONE config home for `llm_retry_max_attempts` / `llm_retry_max_wait_s` (plan body says `LLMConfig`/`[llm]`/`REBAR_LLM_*`; AC + Files say `VerifyConfig`/`[verify]`), update the plan body, AC, Files list, and docs/config.md section to all reference the same schema object, table, and env-var prefix.
findings.finding: The plan body specifies the two new retry config keys on `LLMConfig` (in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, env vars `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS`/`REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`, read from `[llm]` table), while the AC and the Files section both specify them on `VerifyConfig` (in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, prefixed `verify.`, read from `[verify]`). This internal contradiction means the implementer will document, test, and expose config under one schema object while the AC asserts a different one — leaving docs/config.md and operator-facing env vars wrong for whichever side 'loses'.
findings.location: Plan body 'Config keys' section vs AC line 6 and Files section
findings.suggested_fix: Given the plan's own rationale ('every existing LLM OPERATIONAL knob … already lives on LLMConfig'), prefer LLMConfig. Update AC line 6 to read 'llm_retry_max_attempts / llm_retry_max_wait_s exist on LLMConfig, are read from [llm] with REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS / REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S env vars'; update the Files section to name src/rebar/llm/config.py instead of src/rebar/_config_schema.py for these keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the body/AC contradiction: pick ONE home for `llm_retry_max_attempts`/`llm_retry_max_wait_s` — the body's choice (`LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, env `REBAR_LLM_*`, `[llm]` table) is architecturally consistent with every existing LLM operational knob (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) and with the runner's `LLMConfig` parameter; update the AC, Files list, and the env-var names to match whichever choice is made.
findings.finding: The plan body ('Config keys' section) and AC #6 directly contradict each other on where the two new config fields live. The body explicitly assigns `llm_retry_max_attempts` / `llm_retry_max_wait_s` to `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, with env vars `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` / `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`, read from the `[llm]` TOML table. AC #6 instead says `verify.llm_retry_max_attempts` / `verify.llm_retry_max_wait_s` on `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, from `[verify]`. The `Files` list at the bottom names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (VerifyConfig path). A read of both files confirms neither field exists in either class today, so the contradiction is genuine and unresolved — an implementer cannot satisfy both without a clarification.
findings.location: Plan 'Config keys' section vs AC #6 vs Files list
findings.suggested_fix: Keep the body's choice: add the fields to `LLMConfig` (consistent with existing `timeout_s`/`max_tokens`/`max_iterations` precedent and directly accessible inside `PydanticAIRunner.run()`), update AC #6 to say `cfg.llm_retry_max_attempts` / `cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s`, and remove `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` from the Files list (or clarify it's only for a `VerifyConfig` re-export if that is actually intended).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the body-vs-AC6 contradiction: decide authoritatively whether the two retry config keys live on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (body's argument, consistent with `timeout_s`/`max_tokens`/`max_iterations`) or `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (AC6's assertion), and update both the AC and Files list to match the single chosen target.
findings.finding: The plan body (§ 'Config keys') and AC6 name different, mutually exclusive edit targets for the two retry config fields: the body explicitly places `llm_retry_max_attempts` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s` on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (read from `[llm]` TOML via `_llm_int` and `REBAR_LLM_*` env vars), while AC6 asserts `verify.llm_retry_max_attempts` / `verify.llm_retry_max_wait_s` exist on `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (read from `[verify]`). The Files list also names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`. Both files exist; exactly one target is architecturally correct; an implementer cannot know which file to edit without an authoritative resolution.
findings.location: § Config keys + AC6 + Files list
findings.suggested_fix: Accept the body's rationale (it is architecturally correct: `PydanticAIRunner.run()` receives `LLMConfig` and all LLM operational knobs already live there). Update AC6 to read 'exist on `LLMConfig`, are read from `[llm]` table, default 4 / 60; a test sets `llm_retry_max_attempts=1`…' and update the Files list to `src/rebar/llm/config.py` in place of `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between §'Config keys' (keys on LLMConfig, [llm] table) and AC6/Files (keys on VerifyConfig, [verify] table) — pick one location, state it consistently in all three places (body, AC, Files), and confirm the chosen object is in scope where RetryConfig is built inside PydanticAIRunner.run().
findings.finding: The plan body (§'Config keys') and the AC/Files list contradict each other on where the two retry config keys live: the body places them on LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py (REBAR_LLM_* env, [llm] table), while AC6 and the Files list place them on VerifyConfig in src/rebar/_config_schema.py ([verify] table). PydanticAIRunner.run() receives only an LLMConfig — if the keys land on VerifyConfig there is no specified plumbing to bridge them into RetryConfig at construction time, so the mechanism is incomplete.

findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the body's specification — LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py with REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS / REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S — since PydanticAIRunner.run() already receives LLMConfig and every other LLM operational knob (timeout_s, max_tokens, max_iterations) lives there. Update AC6 to say 'llm_retry_max_attempts / llm_retry_max_wait_s exist on LLMConfig, are read from [llm]' and update Files to 'src/rebar/llm/config.py (two LLMConfig fields)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config-key placement contradiction: the body (§ 'Config keys') specifies `LLMConfig`/`[llm]`/`REBAR_LLM_*`, while AC6 and the Files list specify `VerifyConfig`/`[verify]` — pick one location and make the body text, all ACs, env-var names, TOML table, and Files list consistent.
findings.finding: The plan contains a direct internal contradiction on where the two new config keys (`llm_retry_max_attempts`, `llm_retry_max_wait_s`) live: the 'Config keys' body section explicitly places them on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (env vars `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS`/`REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`, read from `[llm]` TOML table via `_llm_int`), while AC6 and the Files list both specify `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with `[verify]` table placement and `verify.` prefixed names. The decision rule for the config owner is unresolved: an executor implementing from the body alone produces `LLMConfig` fields; an executor implementing from the ACs/Files alone produces `VerifyConfig` fields — two incompatible outputs from one plan.
findings.location: § Config keys (body) vs. AC6 vs. Files list
findings.suggested_fix: The body's own rationale is the stronger argument (PydanticAIRunner.run() receives LLMConfig; all LLM operational knobs live there). Correct AC6 and the Files list to read: '`llm_retry_max_attempts` / `llm_retry_max_wait_s` exist on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, read from `[llm]` via `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` / `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`, and default 4 / 60'; remove `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` from the Files list for this change.

findings.finding: `verify.llm_retry_max_attempts` and `verify.llm_retry_max_wait_s` are placed on `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, but all existing LLM operational knobs (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) live on `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`. `VerifyConfig` is in the stdlib core layer which explicitly avoids LLM deps; `PydanticAIRunner.run()` only receives an `LLMConfig`, with no path to `VerifyConfig` — the plan does not specify how the runner reads these values at construction time.
findings.location: What > Config keys (added to VerifyConfig); AC line 6; Files list: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `llm_retry_max_attempts: int = 4` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s: int = 60` to `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, resolved via `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS', 'llm_retry_max_attempts', 4)` in `LLMConfig.from_env()`. Remove from `VerifyConfig` and `_SECTIONS['verify']`.

findings.finding: The plan places `llm_retry_max_attempts` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s` in `VerifyConfig` (a `[verify]` section key in the stdlib-core `_config_schema.py`), but `PydanticAIRunner` — the sole consumer that threads these values into `RetryConfig` — only receives `LLMConfig`. No data-flow path is described from `Config.verify.llm_retry_max_attempts` into `PydanticAIRunner.run()`. The runner's `__init__` takes `LLMConfig`, and `LLMConfig.from_env()` does not read `VerifyConfig`. These two retry-tuning keys are LLM infrastructure settings (not verification policy), making `LLMConfig` / the `[llm]` reserved section their natural home consistent with `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`, and `timeout_s`.
findings.location: What → Config keys section; AC line 6; src/rebar/llm/runner.py (PydanticAIRunner.__init__); src/rebar/_config_schema.py (VerifyConfig, _RESERVED_SECTIONS)
findings.suggested_fix: Move the two keys to `LLMConfig` (read via `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` / `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S` env vars and the `[tool.rebar.llm]` table, consistent with `timeout_s`), update `LLMConfig.from_env()` to read them, and reference them as `cfg.llm_retry_max_attempts` / `cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s` inside `PydanticAIRunner.run()` — matching the existing pattern and requiring no new plumbing.

findings.finding: The plan places `llm_retry_max_attempts` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s` in `VerifyConfig` (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`), but `PydanticAIRunner.run()` receives only an `LLMConfig` object (confirmed: `runner.py:159,183`). No protocol is specified for how the runner retrieves these `VerifyConfig` fields at model-construction time — the decision rule bridging `VerifyConfig` to `RetryConfig` construction is absent from the plan.
findings.location: What → Config keys / What → The single unified Anthropic construction / src/rebar/llm/runner.py:159,183 / src/rebar/_config_schema.py:209-296
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'Config keys' section specifying the access path, e.g.: 'At construction time, `PydanticAIRunner.run()` reads these values by calling `rebar.config.load().verify.llm_retry_max_attempts` (lazy, cached per-run), or alternatively these two keys are mirrored as fields on `LLMConfig` and populated by `LLMConfig.from_env()` reading from the `[verify]` table.' Pick one and state it.

findings.finding: The plan places `llm_retry_max_attempts` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s` in `VerifyConfig` (the stdlib-core `[verify]` section in `_config_schema.py`), but the codebase's established architecture reserves `VerifyConfig` for non-LLM gate-policy booleans/ints, while ALL LLM runtime knobs (`model`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`, `timeout_s`, etc.) live in `LLMConfig` (resolved from the `[llm]` reserved section). `_config_schema.py` line 495 explicitly marks `llm` as a `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` entry 'owned by an optional layer rather than the stdlib core typed Config'. Injecting LLM retry tuning into `VerifyConfig` violates this separation, would cause the stdlib core to know about LLM internals, and is inconsistent with how every other LLM knob is structured.
findings.location: Plan section 'Config keys (added to VerifyConfig)' / `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` VerifyConfig / `src/rebar/llm/config.py` LLMConfig
findings.suggested_fix: Add `llm_retry_max_attempts: int = 4` and `llm_retry_max_wait_s: int = 60` to `LLMConfig` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, and resolve them from `[tool.rebar.llm]` via `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS', 'llm_retry_max_attempts', 4)` and `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S', 'llm_retry_max_wait_s', 60)` in `LLMConfig.from_env()`. Remove the `VerifyConfig` placement from the plan.

findings.finding: The plan body (§ 'Config keys') places llm_retry_max_attempts and llm_retry_max_wait_s on LLMConfig in src/rebar/llm/config.py (with REBAR_LLM_* env vars and [llm] table), while AC6 asserts these same keys 'exist on VerifyConfig', 'are read from [verify]', and uses the prefix 'verify.llm_retry_max_attempts'. findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that the unified retrying-client helper is invoked for `self._model_override is None and resolved.startswith('anthropic')` REGARDLESS of `_direct` (not nested inside `if _direct:`), with `base_url=_direct or None` (where `None` uses the SDK default); explicitly state this restructuring of the `if _direct:` block in the implementation notes.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the normal (non-loopback) anthropic path 'lets pydantic-ai build its own provider internally (SDK default retries, no transport)' — this is confirmed by the code. But the claim that the unified helper will cover BOTH paths (normal + loopback) rests on the loopback branch condition `if _direct:` being the ONLY gate: when no loopback proxy is active (production, CI), `_direct` is falsy and the loopback block is never entered, meaning the plan's unified helper must be invoked UNCONDITIONALLY for any anthropic model before the `if _direct:` conditional — not as a branch of it. The plan's pseudocode shows the helper varying only `base_url`, but does not explicitly state whether it wraps the existing `if self._model_override is None and resolved.startswith('anthropic')` guard or replaces it. If the helper is implemented only inside the `if _direct:` block (the existing branch), the normal path remains un-retried.
findings.location: Plan 'The single unified Anthropic construction' section; runner.py:233-240
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the current `if _direct:` nesting with: `if self._model_override is None and resolved.startswith('anthropic'): _direct = _local_proxy_bypass_base_url() or None; model = <unified helper>(..., base_url=_direct)` — so the retrying client is always built for real anthropic models, with `_direct` varying only the `base_url`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the cross-story AsyncClient merge contract with 'chief-contained-hoopoe': specify which parameter or config field carries the `httpx.Timeout`, where in runner.py the merge point is, and add it to the Files list or Scope if hoopoe modifies runner.py.
findings.finding: The plan names a sibling story ('story chief-contained-hoopoe') that passes its `httpx.Timeout` into the same `httpx.AsyncClient` this story owns and constructs, but 'hoopoe' does not appear in the codebase and is not listed in the Files list or Scope section of this plan. The cross-story interface (who constructs the client, what Timeout value is threaded, and how the merge happens) is described only in a single parenthetical without a concrete parameter name, call site, or merge contract.
findings.location: § Scope, sentence: 'story chief-contained-hoopoe passes its httpx.Timeout INTO this same httpx.AsyncClient'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or test asserting that a non-retriable HTTP status (e.g., 400 or 401) returned by the server is NOT retried and propagates immediately (single attempt only) — this negatively validates the `validate_response` callback's retriable-set boundary.
findings.finding: The test plan has no AC covering that non-retriable HTTP status codes (e.g., 400, 401, 403, 404) are NOT retried by the validate_response callback — a bug in that callback that over-retries auth errors would not be caught by the current test suite.
findings.location: AC item 4 / validate_response callback in "Retry config" section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the spike/unit tests: inject a mock that returns a single 401 response and assert that `requests == 1` (no retry) and the exception propagates as-is. Mirror this as an AC: '401/400 responses are NOT retried; the original HTTP error propagates on the first attempt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly defer to a named child story with rationale) that exercises the unified retrying client against the live Anthropic API — e.g. a smoke test that runs one minimal agent turn with the new construction and confirms `anthropic_client.max_retries == 0` and the transport is active on the actual network path.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the unified retrying client end-to-end against the live Anthropic API after the default-path cutover. All retry proofs (AC2, AC3, AC4, AC5) are offline/mocked sequences. The plan explicitly cuts over the DEFAULT anthropic model path (previously SDK-managed) so that ALL real anthropic model runs now go through the new client construction. Under E6(f), a cutover to a new production default path requires at least one AC that exercises the path as it will run in production (against the live dependency), or an explicit deferral to a named child story with rationale. The plan defers the spike to an offline proof and names no child story for live validation.
findings.location: AC2, AC5, Section 'Spike (the load-bearing proof)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A live smoke test (opt-in, skipped without ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) runs one minimal agent turn through the new unified client construction and asserts `client.max_retries == 0` and the transport wrapper is reachable (e.g. via a counter in a test-double transport that is wired only for this test, confirming the send path passes through the wrapper).' This keeps CI green by default while providing a deterministic live-path proof on demand.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the behavioral change of replacing SDK-default retries with owned transport retry — e.g., describe what operators do if the new transport causes issues (config toggle, revert procedure, or an expand-contract sequence that keeps SDK retries available during rollout).
findings.finding: The plan removes SDK-default retry behavior (previously `max_retries=2` from the Anthropic SDK on both normal and loopback paths) and replaces it with owned transport retry, which is an explicit behavioral change for all real Anthropic calls. While the change is acknowledged and justified, there is no explicit rollback or back-out plan described — the only opt-out is `llm_retry_max_attempts=1` (fail-fast, not a restoration of SDK behavior), and the construction-time guard FAIL-FASTing on injection failure provides no graceful degradation path.
findings.location: Plan body § 'The single unified Anthropic construction', § 'Opt-out semantics', § 'Construction-time guard'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the timeout/retry interaction: clarify whether timeout_s applies per-attempt or to the whole retry sequence, and how the httpx.AsyncClient-level timeout (from hoopoe) and the model_settings['timeout'] (per-request) coexist on the same client — add a note to the §'AsyncClient lifecycle' section and an AC covering the timeout-fires-during-retry edge case.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the per-request timeout (cfg.timeout_s wired via model_settings['timeout']) interacts with tenacity's cumulative retry wait when both are active. With max_attempts=4 and max_wait=60s, the total retry sequence can consume up to ~180s of wait time alone, which may exceed the outer timeout_s=600s default but could also race against a lower operator-configured timeout_s. If the outer pydantic-ai/httpx timeout fires during a tenacity wait interval, it raises an httpx.TimeoutException which is itself in the retry predicate — triggering another retry and resetting the count — or propagates past tenacity unexpectedly.
findings.location: §'Retry config (proven by the spike)' and §'AsyncClient lifecycle'; runner.py:285-286

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a captured spike output (or a dedicated offline unit test) that exercises the `fallback_strategy=None` path with a 500/no-Retry-After->200 sequence and records the actual wait behavior, so the 'tenacity default backoff' claim is verified rather than inferred.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `fallback_strategy=None` in `wait_retry_after` means 'use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present,' but this behavior is not demonstrated by the spike output. The spike only captures a 429+Retry-After->200 PASS. The no-Retry-After code path (what None actually does at runtime when the header is absent) is an unverified capability assertion about library internals.
findings.location: What > Retry config (proven by the spike); AC line: '500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff (so the no-header path is verified, not merely asserted)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the spike (or add a unit test with a mock transport) that fires a 500 with no Retry-After header and asserts: (a) the retry fires at all, (b) the wait is non-zero. Capture this in the experiment log alongside [1]-[6]. If `fallback_strategy=None` does not provide backoff, pass `fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(...)` explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the experiment log (or spike assertions) a captured test that invokes `validate_response` with a synthetic `httpx.Response(status_code=429)` and confirms: (a) the callback signature is `(resp: httpx.Response) -> None`, (b) it raises `httpx.HTTPStatusError` for codes in the retriable set, so the calling convention is proven, not inferred.
findings.finding: The `validate_response` callback's calling convention is unverified. Experiment log [3] confirms the parameter NAME exists on `AsyncTenacityTransport`, but does not confirm the callback receives a single `httpx.Response` argument. If the actual signature is `(request, response)`, `(transport, response)`, or any other arity, the plan's proposed `def validate_response(resp): if resp.status_code in {...}: resp.raise_for_status()` would silently fail at runtime (a TypeError swallowed by the transport's internals, or never called).
findings.location: What > Retry config (proven by the spike): '`validate_response(response)` callback turns retriable STATUS CODES into exceptions'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the spike (tmp/spike.py) to include a `500`(no `Retry-After`)→`200` sequence with `fallback_strategy=None` and record the actual wait behavior (number of seconds, strategy type) in experiment log [7]; update the plan's assertion about `None` semantics to reflect the observed result before dependent AC tests are written.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `fallback_strategy=None` in `wait_retry_after` means 'use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present,' but the spike only exercises the 429+Retry-After path. The no-header fallback behavior (what tenacity does when fallback_strategy=None and no header is present) is an unverified pydantic_ai internal — it could be wait_none (instant retry), a fixed wait, or raise. The AC explicitly requires a `500`(no Retry-After)→`200` test, meaning this path must work, but it was never run in the spike.
findings.location: Retry config section (`fallback_strategy=None` description) and AC line 2 (`a 500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike step that prints `type(resp)` and `inspect.signature(validate_response_callable)` from inside a live `validate_response` invocation during the spike run, and record the confirmed type in experiment log [3b]; update the plan's callback definition to match the confirmed signature.
findings.finding: The `validate_response` callback calling convention is only parameter-name verified (experiment log [3] confirms the parameter exists on AsyncTenacityTransport) but the type and shape of the argument passed to the callback is not confirmed. The plan proposes `def validate_response(resp): if resp.status_code in {...}: resp.raise_for_status()` assuming `resp` is an `httpx.Response`. If the callback receives a different type (e.g. a raw bytes buffer, a pydantic_ai wrapper, or the callback is invoked with (request, response) — two args) the validate_response body will raise an AttributeError at runtime.
findings.location: Retry config section — `validate_response(response)` callback definition

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike log line that records the exact import statement used (e.g. `from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport, wait_retry_after, RetryConfig  # OK`) and include the full dotted path in the plan's Dependency section so the implementation has an unambiguous import target.
findings.finding: The plan states the import path `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and `pydantic_ai.retries.wait_retry_after` without the experiment log confirming the full dotted module path. Log [1] confirms the extra exists; log [2]/[3] confirm parameter names; but no log line shows `from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport` succeeding. The actual module could be `pydantic_ai._utils.retries`, `pydantic_ai.providers._retry`, or another internal path — a wrong import path causes an `ImportError` at runner construction time.
findings.location: Dependency section: 'ships `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` + `wait_retry_after`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the experiment log: `[7] from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport, wait_retry_after, RetryConfig: OK  (module path: pydantic_ai.retries)`

findings.finding: The ADR stub is targeted at `docs/adr/0026-transport-retry.md`, but the `0026` prefix is already occupied by TWO existing ADRs: `docs/adr/0026-autodeploy-on-box-timer.md` and `docs/adr/0026-reconciler-three-way-merge-baseline.md`. The next available number based on the directory listing (which goes up to `0036-*`) would be `0037` or higher.
findings.location: What > ADR; AC line 7; Files list: `docs/adr/0026-transport-retry.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `docs/adr/0026-transport-retry.md` with `docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md` (or whatever the actual next free number is) throughout the plan.

findings.finding: The ADR placeholder path `docs/adr/0026-transport-retry.md` collides with the existing live ADR `docs/adr/0026-autodeploy-on-box-timer.md` (confirmed in the repository). Creating the stub at the specified path would shadow or overwrite a live accepted ADR.
findings.location: ADR section / docs/adr/0026-transport-retry.md (placeholder) / docs/adr/0026-autodeploy-on-box-timer.md (existing live file)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ADR path to `docs/adr/00NN-transport-retry.md` with an explicit note that the owner assigns the actual number, or use `0037` (the next free slot visible in the directory listing).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
